coredata.dc:title: Tunable multi-peak emission solid-state fluorescent carbon dots: Luminescence regulation mechanism and application in single-component-based LEDs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yong, Chao
Lei, Ying
Liu, Yinghui
Li, Yu
Wang, Nan
Ye, Fan
Chen, Hongwei
coredata.dc:description: The design of solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with multi-peak emission has become an urgent challenge to be solved with the in-depth research of CDs in LEDs. Here, one- and two-component solid-state white light CDs are synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using the biological pollutant cyanobacteria, respectively. The solid-state fluorescence quantum yield of the one-component CDs was as high as 41.8 %, and the solid-state fluorescence mechanism of CDs is discussed to be mainly attributed to the self-quenching-resistant feature of “core-shell” structure and the reabsorption/RET-induced long-wavelength red light emission, and attenuation of the short emission wavelength. For two-component white light CDs, blue- and red-light CDs are obtained by simple dialysis separation, realizing the fluorescence tunability. The different origins of their multi-peak emission are discussed: core, edge and surface states. The synthesis yields of CDs are up to 62 %, enabling stable gram-scale production. The white- and red-light CDs are used as phosphors to fabricate fluorescent films and cold, pure, and warm white- and red-light LEDs with good resistance to photobleaching and temperature stability and CRI of 84.4, 85.7 and 61.5.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Core-shell structure
Multi-peak emission
Band-gap tuning
WLEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of WLEDs from N-WCDs and B, N-WCDs
Synthesis and characterizations of multicolor fluorescent CDs
Optical properties of multicolor fluorescent CDs
Synthesis and fluorescent mechanism of multicolor fluorescent CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis of CDs.
TEM, HRTEM images (inset) and particle size distribution statistics of N-WCDs (a, e), B, N-BCDs (b, f), B, N-WCDs (c, g) and B, N-RCDs (d, h).
Raman spectra (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of CDs.
The full range XPS spectra and C1s, N1s, O1s and B1s spectra of N-WCDs (a), B, N-BCDs (b), B, N-WCDs (c) and B, N-RCDs (d).
PL spectra of aqueous solution and solid powder of N-WCDs (a, b), B, N-WCDs (c, d) and B, N-RCDs (e, f). Inset: photographs of liquid and solid CDs under 365 nm UV irradiation.
UV-Vis spectra of aqueous solutions (a) and solid powders (b) of N-WCDs, B, N-WCDs, B, N-BCDs and B, N-RCDs, the inset is a magnified image at 600 nm, and fluorescence decay curves (c, d).
Schematic structure models (a) and energy level (b) of CDs.
The comparison of normalized PL spectra for N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs aqueous solution (a) and powder (b).
Images of prepared CDs/epoxy films under daylight (a) and UV light (b).

EL spectra, CIE color coordinates and colorful balls photographs of WLEDs based on N-WCDs (a, b, c) and B, N-WCDs (d, e, f), the insets are photographs of the WLEDs operated at 3.5 V and commercial UV chips (400 nm), respectively.
Luminous spectra and color rendering index of N-WCDs (a, b) and B, N-WCDs (c, d) with different driving currents (20–320 mA).

head.ce:title: Tunable multi-peak emission solid-state fluorescent carbon dots: Luminescence regulation mechanism and application in single-component-based LEDs

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical Engineering, Tongling University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with multi-peak emission has become an urgent challenge to be solved with the in-depth research of CDs in LEDs. Here, one- and two-component solid-state white light CDs are synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using the biological pollutant cyanobacteria, respectively. The solid-state fluorescence quantum yield of the one-component CDs was as high as 41.8 %, and the solid-state fluorescence mechanism of CDs is discussed to be mainly attributed to the self-quenching-resistant feature of “core-shell” structure and the reabsorption/RET-induced long-wavelength red light emission, and attenuation of the short emission wavelength. For two-component white light CDs, blue- and red-light CDs are obtained by simple dialysis separation, realizing the fluorescence tunability. The different origins of their multi-peak emission are discussed: core, edge and surface states. The synthesis yields of CDs are up to 62 %, enabling stable gram-scale production. The white- and red-light CDs are used as phosphors to fabricate fluorescent films and cold, pure, and warm white- and red-light LEDs with good resistance to photobleaching and temperature stability and CRI of 84.4, 85.7 and 61.5.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are burgeoning nanomaterials with distinctive optical properties, good biocompatibility, low cost, environmental friendliness, facile functionalization, and high photostability [1–4]. The exceptional properties have stimulated the interest of many researchers in conducting further studies and demonstrated the broad application prospects of CDs in various fields, including biofuel, sensors, medical imaging, energy storage, catalyst, and LEDs luminescence [5–13]. While tunable multicolor luminescence of CDs in the liquid state has been achieved in current research, solid-state CDs always undergo fluorescence self-quenching due to two main factors: excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct π-π interactions [14,15]. The inherent hygroscopic properties of CDs further exacerbate this issue, posing a significant challenge to the controllable preparation of solid-state fluorescence (SSF) CDs. Consequently, this limitation significantly restricts the application range of CDs. The limited number of solid fluorescent CDs cases [16,17] lacks a detailed analysis of their emission mechanism and fails to provide specific explanations for the anti-self-quenching phenomena observed in CDs powder. In particular, biomass-based CDs suffer from a highly uncontrollable nature due to their complex biomass source, making it difficult to achieve precise chemical treatment and microstructure modulation during the synthesis process [18]. As a result, the potential fluorescence emission mechanism of biomass-based CDs is difficult to analyze effectively, and the fluorescence quantum yield is generally low, thus inhibiting further expansion of research on functionalized biomass-based CDs. On the other hand, researchers are increasingly favoring cheap, easy-to-obtain, abundant and environmentally friendly natural biomass and biowaste carbon sources, leading to the emergence of biomass-based CDs as a new trend in CDs research [19,20]. Kalita et al. [21] synthesized monodisperse CDs of different sizes (2–6.5 nm) by controlling the heating time using rice grains as a carbon source. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was highly dependent on the size, with an increase in QY from 16 % to 24 % as the size was reduced from 6.5 to 2 nm. Yang et al. [22] successfully prepared red-light emitting biomass-based CDs by a one-pot hydrothermal method using waste tobacco leaves as a biomass precursor, and applied the CDs to the detection and removal of Hg2+, with a detection limit of 0.45 μM and a high mercury removal efficiency of more than 99.4 %. Due to the properties of cheap, low toxicity, green and tunable optical characteristics, biomass-based CDs are even more highly desirable alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots or organic dyes for bioimaging and drug delivery [23,24]. Nevertheless, biomass-based solid fluorescent CDs continue to be rarely reported. Therefore, it is imperative to develop novel functionalized anti-self-quenching biomass-based CDs to expand their competitive advantages in emerging applications and systematically elucidate their SSF mechanisms.

White-light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have garnered considerable attention and research because of their low energy consumption, extended lifespan, high luminous efficacy, environmental friendliness, and their potential to replace incandescent and fluorescent lamps as the next generation of lighting systems. In recent years, CDs have been highly emphasized by researchers in this field due to their tunable fluorescence, high efficiency, and affordability. Currently, CDs-based WLEDs remain in the early phase of investigation, and the majority of synthesized CDs exhibit blue light emission characterized with low fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) and do not emit light in the solid state [25]. In the pursuit of WLEDs, blue-light-emitting CDs are usually combined with green- and red-light-emitting semiconductor quantum dots [26,27]. And to counteract the SSF self-quenching, it is usually necessary to disperse the CDs in other substrates such as starch [28], silica [29] and epoxy resin [30]. These methodologies complicate the synthesis process while significantly diminishing the inherent advantages of CDs, namely, their low toxicity, inexpensive and green. Mia et al. [31] mixed the synthesized blue, green, and red fluorescent CDs in a 1:3:2 mass ratio and compounded them with epoxy resin to achieve a WLEDs conversion layer. Due to the distinct characteristics of CDs synthesized from varying processes and carbon sources, the method still encounters issues such as self-absorption, poor reproducibility and compromised fluorescence stability. Lu et al. [32] prepared ultra-small white light CDs (∼0.5 nm) using L-serine and L-tryptophan as carbon sources and controlled the pH of the solution using the one-pot method. And the synthesis of white light CDs was also successfully achieved using ionic liquid passivation by Mehra et al. [33]. However, the emission peaks of these CDs have only one broad peak, which leads to poor controllability and coordination and makes it difficult to achieve truly pure white light emission. Hence, it is urgent to explore universal methods and theories to rationally design the structure of CDs and modulate their surface groups, with this enabling the realization of white light emitting CDs with multicolor emission bands while further improving the QYs.

Based on our published work [18], blue-, white- and red-light CDs (B, N-BCDs, B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs) with a "core-shell" structure are designed and synthesized using cyanobacteria by B doping and alkali passivation. The multi-peak emission and fluorescence tunability of biomass-based CDs were successfully realized. Notably, the pure cyanobacterial CDs (N-WCDs) and B, N-WCDs showed bright white light emission in the solid state, and B, N-RCDs are red-light. WLEDs with efficient fluorescence emission were successfully prepared. Moreover, the different sources of solid-liquid fluorescence emission and the fluorescence redshift mechanism of “core-shell” CDs were discussed.
Cyanobacteria were obtained from rivers in southern China and dried and crushed. NaOH and H3BO3 were purchased from Shanghai Macklin, and the deionized water ≥18.25 MΩ·cm.

The fluorescence lifetimes were measured by the FLS1000 spectrometer, and fluorescence spectra were analyzed by a PerkinElmer LS55 spectrometer. UV–Vis spectra were recorded with UV3600. The quantum yield of fluorescence (QYs) was obtained from the FLS980 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) photographs were taken on the JEM-2100 electron microscope. The functional groups were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Nicolet6700 spectrometer). Raman spectra were obtained from the HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained with Thermo Scientific K-Alpha.

Cyanobacteria powder, H3BO3 and NaOH were weighed in the ratio of 10:3:3 and configured as an aqueous solution, which was heated in the microwave oven at 700 W for 10 min until the water evaporated. The carbonation products were dispersed by sonication with the appropriate amount of deionized water and then centrifuged three times at 12000 r/min for 18 min. One part of the centrifuged supernatant was saved for subsequent freeze-drying for 48 h to obtain B, N-WCDs, and the other part was dialyzed with 500 Da dialysis bag for 60 h. The B, N-RCDs inside the dialysis bag and the B, N-BCDs outside the bag were thoroughly separated. Finally, the two kinds of CDs were freeze-dried separately for 48 h to obtain CDs powder. The aqueous solution of pure cyanobacteria was prepared and the above experimental procedure was repeated to obtain N-WCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of WLEDs from N-WCDs and B, N-WCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of CDs and epoxy resin were mixed evenly (usually 3:10), and the powder glue mixture was coated on the LED chips. The LEDs were cured at 120 °C for 2 h. Among them, the epoxy resin and 365 nm ultraviolet LED chip were purchased from Shenzhen Xusheng Electronic Technology Co., Ltd. The spectra, color coordinates and other photoelectric properties of LEDs are mainly measured by OHSP-350 M LED Fast-Scan Spectrophotometer. In addition, to prepare the luminescent films, CDs were thoroughly mixed with epoxy resin at a mass ratio of 1:15 and placed in molds, and then the molds were peeled off after drying at room temperature for 5 h to obtain the films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of multicolor fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the synthesis of CDs. With cyanobacteria as the carbon source, monolithic N-WCDs and B, N co-doped hybrid CDs (B, N-WCDs) were synthesized by microwave. And the synthesis yields of CDs are 40 % and 62 %, enabling stable gram-scale production. N-WCDs aqueous solution and powder show blue and white light emission, respectively. Whereas, both solutions and powders of B, N co-doped CDs exhibit white light emission, and blue CDs (B, N-BCDs) and red CDs (B, N-RCDs) are further isolated by dialysis. This indicates that the white light of the hybrid CDs is obtained by the joint contribution of B, N-BCDs and B, N-RCDs, realizing the tunability of white light and laying the foundation for cool, pure and warm WLEDs. The fluorescence variance needs to be further analyzed for the graphitization, quantum size and surface structure of CDs.

Fig. 2(a)-(d) show the TEM, HRTEM images and particle size statistics of CDs, and all CDs are uniformly distributed. The crystal plane spacing of N-WCDs, B, N-BCDs, B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs are 0.206 nm, 0.212 nm, 0.210 nm and 0.212 nm, which are all attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [34]. The average sizes are 4.14 nm, 3.82 nm, 4.01 nm and 4.24 nm, respectively, with a narrow size distribution of 2–6.25 nm. All CDs have nearly the same particle size and crystal plane spacing, it can be speculated that the quantum size effect is not the main reason for their fluorescence difference.

Fig. 3(a) shows the Raman spectra, and all four CDs have a D peak (1348 cm−1) representing the disordered surfaces and a G peak (1577 cm−1) of the graphite networks. The ID/IG intensity ratios of N-WCDs, B, N-BCDs, B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs are 1.02, 1.02, 1.03 and 1.05, which indicates that the degree of graphitization of the CDs is also not the main reason for their having large fluorescence difference. FT-IR spectra show that all CDs have –OH at 3418 cm−1, –NH at 3266 cm−1, CO/CN at 1648 cm−1, C–N at 1543 cm−1, and secondary amide at 1235 cm−1 (Fig. 3b) [25–37]. Compared to N-WCDs, the –OH stretching strength of B, N co-doped CDs is significantly increased by alkali passivation, indicating that alkali makes the surface of CDs covered with numerous hydroxyl groups. Interestingly, the maximum C–N stretching intensities of B, N-RCDs confirm that B, N-RCDs contain the most graphitic nitrogen [38]. The B, N co-doped CDs have vibrational peaks of B–OH, C–B, and N–B at 968 cm−1, 1094 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 [39,40], further demonstrating the successful doping of boron atoms into the CDs.

The structure and surface functionalization of CDs are further studied by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 4. The full-scan spectra calculations are shown in Table S1, and B is successfully doped into the CDs. And B, N co-doping increases the N content of CDs and reduces the O content, demonstrating that the dehydration condensation process of B, N co-doped CDs is intensified [18]. According to the C1s spectra, all CDs have four peaks representing C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C–N (286.7 eV), C–O/C–B (287.9 eV) and CO (288.9 eV) [41]. The content statistics of the C1s spectra are shown in Table S2, with the C–C/CC content of all CDs approaching 50 %. This indicates that cyanobacteria gradually form a graphitized core during carbonation, consistent with TEM analysis. As the reaction proceeds, massive amounts of –OH and amide groups are cross-linked on its surface to form a “groups shell”, and the “core-shell”-like structure is known as fluorescent CDs. The “core-shell” structure prevents excessive proximity of “core” to “core” in the solid aggregation state of CDs, and maintains the self-quenching-resistant feature of CDs powders [42].

The N1s spectra of B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs possess three peaks corresponding to amino N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N [43,44]. N-WCDs and B, N-BCDs had only two peaks of pyrrolic N and graphitic N, in which pyrrolic N dominates. As the fluorescence of CDs shifts from blue to red, the percentage of pyrrolic N drops dramatically and that of graphitic N gradually improves (Table S3), signifying the important role of graphite N for fluorescence redshift. It corresponds to the analysis of FT-IR spectra. The O1s spectra of all four CDs have CO bonds (531.6 eV) and C–OH bonds (532.9 eV) [45], with N-WCDs also having C–O–C bonds (533.6 eV) and B, N-BCDs and B, N-WCDs having additional COOH groups (535.2 eV), possibly related to boric acid. The variety of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of BN-RCDs is relatively few and the C–OH bond content considerably enhances with the fluorescence redshift of CDs, which are 20.55 %, 27.56 %, 35.84 %, and 56.77 % (Table S4). It shows that specific oxygen-containing functional groups –OH can achieve effective fluorescence redshift. Based on the B1s spectra, it can be seen that three B, N co-doped CDs have three peaks corresponding to N–B (190.2 eV), O–B (191.0 eV) and C–B (192.1 eV) [39,46]. It further confirms the analysis results of FT-IR spectra and proves that the B-doping of CDs is successfully achieved (Table S5). B doping alters the bonding between C and N, and functional groups like N–B, O–B and C–B promote the formation of edge states, providing the possibility of multiple “core-shell” structures for CDs [47] and corresponding to their multi-peak fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of multicolor fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs aqueous solution and powder and their photographs under 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), the liquid N-WCDs have the highest fluorescence emission peak at 447 nm excited at 360 nm, showing strong blue fluorescence. While in solid state, the N-WCDs display multiple fluorescence emission peaks at 460 nm, 680 nm and 728 nm, thus producing white light emission. The aqueous solutions of B, N co-doped CDs all have three peaks located at 458 nm, 662 nm and 712 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(c), (e) and S1. The blue, white and red fluorescence is attributed to the two distinct emission centers at 458 nm and 662 nm. The B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs powders exhibit strong white- and red-light emission, as shown in Fig. 5(d), (f). Interestingly, their PL spectra in the solid state are similar to that of N-WCDs, with only two additional emission peaks between 500 and 650 nm, which further achieves the white-light tunable multi-peak emission in the solid state. The SSF color coordinate of N-WCDs is (0.31, 0.30) as shown in Figure S2, and B, N-WCDs achieve precise modulation of color coordinates from cool to warm white light through the regulation of boric acid and alkali content (e.g. alkali: boric acid = 0.6:1, 0.7:1, 0.8:1, 0.9:1, 1:1, 1.1:1, 1.15:1, 1.2:1, 1.25:1, 1.3:1), such as (0.33,.33) for pure white light emission. It is noteworthy that all CDs have excitation-independent properties, indicating that CDs feature relatively uniform particle size and a homogeneous surface structure [48]. The solid-state absolute fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) for N-WCDs, B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs are 41.8 %, 11.4 % and 11.3 %, respectively.

Fig. 6 shows the UV–vis spectra of aqueous solutions and powders of CDs. The aqueous solutions of the four CDs have obvious absorption peaks at 265 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of sp2-hybridised carbon on the carbon core of CDs [49]. B, N co-doped CDs have three additional peaks at 402, 500 and 663 nm, attributed to the n-π* transitions of CO, C–N and CN, respectively [50,51]. The intensity of the three peaks gradually enhances with the fluorescence from blue, white to red, indicating that B, N co-doping modulates the structure and surface state of CDs to achieve fluorescence redshift. This is consistent with the absorption peaks of B, N co-doped CDs in the solid state. Compared to the UV–vis spectra of the liquid phase, the N-WCDs solid powder exhibits additional weak absorption peaks at 402 nm and 663 nm, attributing to π-π stacking in the solid aggregated state [52]. The broad absorption results in reabsorption and tends to induce extensive emission, raising the possibility of multi-peak emission. The redshift of absorption spectra also reveals a smaller bandgap for CDs powders, and the reduced bandgap enables further fluorescence redshift [37].

To further analyze the white light emission of N-WCDs powder, the fluorescence decay curves of N-WCDs aqueous solution and powder were tested (Fig. 6c). The fluorescence lifetimes are 3.63 ns and 2.40 ns, respectively. It is due to the competition of RET with radiative transition, shortening the lifetime [53]. This indicates that N-WCDs solid aggregates induce the RET. In summary, we speculate that the white light of N-WCDs powders can be attributed to the self-quenching-resistant feature and the reabsorption/RET-induced long-wavelength red light emission, and attenuation of the short emission wavelength (400–500 nm). The fluorescence lifetimes of B, N co-doped CDs are presented in Fig. 6(d). All three CDs show a double-exponential decay (Table S6), containing the τ1 component associated with the carbon core and the τ2 component associated with the surface defective states, which indicates that the fluorescence emission of the CDs is jointly contributed by the graphitic core and the surface defective states [18]. With the redshift of B, N co-doped CDs (from blue, white to red), the proportion of τ1 gradually decreases, while τ2 accounts for the progressive increase, and the proportion of τ2 reaches 65.82 % in B, N-RCDs. This proves that the red-light emission of B, N co-doped CDs mainly depends on the surface defect states. The fluorescence lifetimes are 2.22 ns, 2.27 ns, and 2.30 ns, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and fluorescent mechanism of multicolor fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The possible synthesis process of CDs could be summarized from the structural characterization and fluorescence difference analysis: firstly, cyanobacteria undergo the dehydration and condensation reaction under microwave irradiation to form cross-linked polymers. As the reaction continues, some of the polymers further carbonize to form denser carbon cores, while the remaining part is cross-linked with other organic long-chain molecules and encapsulated on the surface of the carbon core, thereby forming the most primitive “core-shell” structure ( Fig. 7a). The polymer shell-like structure prevents excessive proximity of " core " to " core " in the solid aggregation state of CDs, reducing the effect of RET or direct π-π interactions, and maintaining the self-quenching-resistant feature of CDs powders [42]. On this basis, B, N co-doping and alkali passivation exacerbate the carbonation of carbon core and dehydration condensation reaction (Table S2), and introduce more subfluorophores such as amide, C–B, N–B, and –OH, which enables the B, N co-doped CDs to have three emission mechanisms, core, edge, and surface states [54], and obtaining red-emitting CDs (B, N-RCDs) with multiple peaks of emission. The edge state is mainly attributed to amide, C–B, N–B, while the surface state is mainly attributed to –OH [18,55]. When the CD is excited in the UV, radiative recombination of π-π* excitons within the carbon core produces blue light emission. Additionally, the n-π*-like absorption associated with the edge states predominates, and together with the excitons diffusing from the carbon core, these radiatively recombine at sub-bandgap energies to produce edge-state emission [56]. The edge states emit fluorescence at energies below the bandgap, which in general is significantly more redshifted than the prior emission and may exhibit green or yellow fluorescence [57]. This process constitutes the non-radiative decay of the excitation core to the edge state. Furthermore, both the excitation core and the edge state undergo non-radiative decay to the energy level of surface fluorescent groups, leading to the production of lower-energy surface fluorescence, which results in red light emission [58,59]. As shown in Figs. 8 and 6, compared with N-WCDs, B, N co-doped CDs have four additional/enhanced fluorescence emission peaks in the range of 550–650 nm and 650–750 nm, as well as the three additional absorption peaks, which validate the contribution of the edge and surface states to fluorescence emission. Remarkably, with the redshift of CDs, the fluorescence lifetime τ1 decreases from 100 % to 34.17 %, while τ2 increases from 0 % to 65.82 % (Table S6). This change further confirms the above-mentioned observation. In the final stage of the synthesis reaction, alkali and boric acid are depleted and a stable cross-condensation reaction cannot be maintained, leading to the dominance of carbon core state emission. This results in the production of blue-emitting CDs (B, N-BCDs) dominated by the carbon core state. Therefore, the B, N-WCDs are hybrid white CDs containing blue- and red-emitting CDs.

It is necessary to clarify the stability/durability of CDs to explore their potential application in LEDs. As shown in Figure S5, the PL spectra of N-WCDs, B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs powders do not undergo significant changes after 10 hours of exposure to UV light, indicating the good photobleaching resistance of CDs. Due to the working environment of LEDs devices generally being around 120 °C, the thermogravimetric curves of CDs in a nitrogen atmosphere are tested (Figure S6). The three CDs only begin to progressively lose weight after 200°C, which meets the requirements of LED phosphors. The loss of weight could be attributed to more oxygen-containing groups of CDs.

We first synthesize CDs/epoxy composite films ( Fig. 9). It can be found that the films have good transmittance under daylight and show efficient white and red fluorescence under UV light, demonstrating that color-tunable CDs will be the most promising alternative to LEDs. Therefore, WLEDs based on N-WCDs and B, N-WCDs powders (N-WCDs-WLEDs, B, N-WCDs-WLEDs) are successfully constructed with the high color rendering index (CRI) of 84.4 and 85.7. The electroluminescence (EL) spectra of N-WCDs-WLEDs and B, N-WCDs-WLEDs show bimodal and trimodal emission ( Fig. 10(a), d), corresponding to the PL spectra of CDs powders. The CIE (Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage 1931) color coordinates (Fig. 10b, e) are (0.33, 0.32) and (0.34, 0.33) respectively, and the color temperatures are 5426 K and 4890 K, which are very close to the pure WLEDs (0.33, 0.33), and render the original color of the colored balls perfectly (Fig. 10c, f). Fig. 11 shows the EL spectra and CRI of WLEDs with different driving currents. It can be found that the EL spectra increase almost linearly with the driving current from 20 mA to 320 mA, and the spectral characteristics remain almost unchanged, and the change in CRI is very minimal. The CIE color coordinates change to (0.349, 0.323) and (0.348, 0.324), and the color temperatures change to 4664 K and 4786 K. This indicates that the two WLEDs exhibit excellent color and light stability. Furthermore, B, N-WCDs-based cold and warm WLEDs are also successfully prepared by modulating the content of boric acid and alkali, as shown in Figure S7. As shown in Table S7, the cyanobacteria-based CDs have higher QYs than other CDs-based WLEDs, and the prepared WLEDs are closer to the CIE and colour temperatures of pure white LEDs, which satisfy the illumination needs in most occasions. What's more, the prepared WLEDs have higher CIR and can achieve stable and efficient white light emission [61,62]. Red light LEDs are further prepared for B, N-RCDs, as shown in Figure S8. The CIE color coordinate is (0.38, 0.27), and the color temperature and CRI reach 4331 K and 61.5, respectively. In conclusion, LEDs manufactured directly from CDs powder simplify the preparation process, reduce the cost, improve the light conversion efficiency and avoid the uncertainty of light conversion.

N-WCDs and B, N-WCDs with “core-shell” structure are synthesized in one step by microwave using cyanobacteria as carbon source. The B, N-WCDs are further separated by simple dialysis into two single-component blue and red fluorescent CDs (B, N-BCDs, B, N-RCDs). N-WCDs, B, N-WCDs and B, N-RCDs powders exhibit strong white and red SSF, whereas in water they show blue, white and red fluorescence, respectively. The multi-peak emission and fluorescence tunability of biomass-based CDs are successfully realized, and the different origins of their multi-peak emission are discussed: core, edge and surface states. The fluorescence difference between N-WCDs aqueous solution and powder is mainly attributed to the self-quenching-resistant feature of the “core-shell” structure and the reabsorption/RET-induced long-wavelength red light emission, and attenuation of the short emission wavelength (400–500 nm). Finally, these three CDs are used as phosphors to manufacture cold, pure, and warm white- and red-light LEDs with good resistance to photobleaching and temperature stability with CRI of 84.4, 85.7 and 61.5.

Fan Ye: Resources. Hongwei Chen: Resources. Nan Wang: Validation, Resources. Yinghui Liu: Validation, Resources, Investigation. Yu Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Chao Yong: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ying Lei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51974005), Tongling University talent research start-up fund project (Grant No. 2023tlxyrc22), Youth Foundation of Anhui University of Technology (Grant No. QZ202210).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.176051.
